name: Inchcolm Island
title: The Firth of Forth
note: Pictures for the big-picture opening: the great sea inlet, its island abbey, and the mighty Forth Bridge.

items.caption: The Forth Bridge, one of the wonders of the industrial world, leaping across the great sea inlet. A firth is a broad estuary where a river widens into the sea.
items.kidcaption: The gigantic red Forth Bridge, one of the most amazing bridges ever built, striding right across the water on legs like a giant.

items.caption: Inchcolm Abbey, the 'Iona of the East'. For centuries the firth's little islands served as monasteries, fortresses and prisons, each with its own job.
items.kidcaption: An 800-year-old abbey on a tiny island, where monks once lived. The islands out here have been churches, forts, and even prisons!

items.caption: The Firth of Forth, the sea road that shaped Edinburgh and eastern Scotland: a highway for monks, traders, kings and warships alike.
items.kidcaption: Look out over the wide Firth of Forth. For a thousand years this water was a busy road for boats of monks, traders, and even Viking raiders.
